title: Obstetric Fistula
chief_complaint: I had a difficult labor 6 months ago in my village. My baby was stillborn after 3 days of labor. Since then, I leak urine constantly, day and night. I can't control it at all. My husband left me and my family keeps me isolated.

physical_exam.General: Thin, malnourished. Urine-soaked clothing. Ammonia odor (urine scalding of perineum and inner thighs).
physical_exam.Pelvic: Large vesicovaginal fistula: 4cm defect in anterior vaginal wall communicating with bladder. Bladder mucosa visible through defect. Continuous leakage of urine from fistula. Cervix and uterus normal. No rectovaginal fistula (no fecal incontinence). Perineal skin excoriated from constant moisture.
physical_exam.Dye_test: Methylene blue dye instilled into bladder via Foley catheter: Blue dye immediately leaks from anterior vaginal wall defect. Confirms vesicovaginal fistula.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Normal lower extremity exam. Normal perineal sensation.

hidden_data.cystoscopy: Large 4cm vesicovaginal fistula in supratrigonal position. Ureteral orifices intact (not involved in fistula). Bladder capacity moderately reduced (prolonged disuse). No bladder calculi.
hidden_data.iv_pyelogram: Normal upper urinary tracts. No ureteral involvement. No hydronephrosis.
hidden_data.cbc: Hb 8.5 (iron deficiency anemia - malnutrition, chronic disease), WBC 7,500, Platelets 280,000

correct_diagnosis: Obstetric Vesicovaginal Fistula from Prolonged Obstructed Labor with Fetal Demise
key_learning_points: Obstetric fistula = abnormal communication between vagina and bladder (vesicovaginal) or rectum (rectovaginal) from prolonged obstructed labor. Pressure necrosis from impacted fetal head compresses soft tissues against pubic symphysis → ischemia → necrosis → fistula formation 5-10 days postpartum

Epidemiology: 2-3 million women worldwide (mostly sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia). Largely eliminated in developed countries with access to emergency obstetric care. Risk factors: Young age (<18 - immature pelvis), First pregnancy, Malnutrition (contracted pelvis), Lack of skilled birth attendants, No access to C-section, Poverty

Presentation: Continuous urinary incontinence (day and night - total incontinence if large fistula). May also have: Foot drop (common peroneal nerve compression during prolonged labor), Amenorrhea, Vaginal stenosis, Infertility (endometritis), Psychological trauma (depression, suicide risk, social isolation, divorce, stigma - 'women who smell')

Diagnosis: Clinical (history of prolonged obstructed labor + continuous incontinence). Speculum exam: Visualization of fistula. Dye test: Methylene blue instilled into bladder → leaks from vagina (confirms VVF). Double-dye test if suspecting ureterovaginal fistula. Cystoscopy to evaluate fistula size, location, and relation to ureteral orifices. Evaluate for RVF (rectovaginal) and ureteral involvement

Treatment: SURGICAL REPAIR. Timing: Delay 3-6 months after delivery (allows inflammation to resolve, necrotic tissue to declare itself - improves success). Principles: Wide mobilization of bladder from vagina, Excision of fibrotic fistula edges, Tension-free closure in multiple layers (bladder in 2 layers, vagina in 1 layer - avoid overlapping suture lines), Interposition graft if large/recurrent (Martius labial fat pad graft - brings new blood supply). Continuous bladder drainage ×10-14 days post-op. Success rate: 80-95% for first repair by experienced surgeon. Post-op: Avoid intercourse ×3 months, Deliver future pregnancies by C-section. Complex fistulas may require multiple surgeries, urinary diversion

Prevention: Access to family planning, Delaying first pregnancy, Skilled birth attendants at every delivery, Access to emergency obstetric care (C-section), Partograph use (identifies prolonged labor early), Adequate nutrition (prevents contracted pelvis). Organizations: Fistula Foundation, Hamlin Fistula Ethiopia (Dr. Catherine Hamlin), UNFPA Campaign to End Fistula
